<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: governance-rights-fork-exit-and-audit
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/governance-rights-fork-exit-and-audit.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/governance-rights-fork-exit-and-audit.qmd@761aa06dc
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Governance Rights: Fork, Exit, and Audit

A right becomes real at the moment it becomes inconvenient.

The previous chapter gave unresolved value conflict a durable record. That is
not enough. A record can exist and still be trapped inside the system that
created it. The affected person may be unable to inspect it, challenge it,
leave with enough continuity to keep working, or fork the project without
losing the very evidence that justified dissent.

Governance rights are the layer that prevents moral residuals from becoming
decorative. Audit, exit, fork, appeal, and dissent are not only ideals. In the
ASI Stack they are interfaces: request paths, receipts, evidence bundles,
redaction rules, preservation obligations, appeal routes, and safety limits.

The test is practical. Does the right still work when the system operator
would rather it did not? If a policy update, memory migration, model
replacement, or governance transition can quietly remove the path, the right
was never part of the architecture. It was a sentence in a policy document.

## Problem

Powerful AI infrastructure needs rights and mechanisms that prevent governance
lock-in and hidden control.

The stack is meant to plan, remember, reason, route work, use tools, revise
beliefs, and improve its own components. That makes governance a runtime
problem. A person should not have to trust the same opaque authority that made
the contested decision, holds the logs, controls the export path, defines the
appeal process, and decides whether a fork is allowed.

Value conflict makes this sharper. If a system records disagreement but keeps
the record behind one authority boundary, it has not made disagreement
contestable. It has archived dissent in a room the affected party cannot
enter.

The chapter's source family supplies the vocabulary. Ethica Mechanica gives
the strongest reviewed lineage for contestable recursive governance, public
correction, agency, institutional memory, fork, exit, audit, and hard-coded
rights. Coherence Exchange contributes useful connector-side framing around
fork, exit, audit, contestability, verification supply chains, and governance
interfaces, but it remains source-note mapped rather than line-addressed in a
local raw cache here. Alignment Field and the AI Constitution supply
non-domination, agency, consent, reversibility, auditability, least sufficient
power, and self-improvement review constraints. Ladon/Manhattan contributes
the authority-boundary lesson: credential use, permission lookup, and
execution compartments have to be inspectable without revealing the secrets
they protect. Spinoza and UAT contribute claim-revision and adversarial-review
discipline.

Those sources do not prove that this repository has built a legitimate
institution. They justify a design requirement: governance rights need
technical form.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Transparency is too weak.

A system can show an explanation while withholding the logs that matter. It
can publish a policy while hiding the appeal path. It can permit exit while
keeping the memory, provenance, tools, identity, or project graph that makes
exit materially usable. It can permit fork while discarding safety obligations
or source lineage. It can allow dissent while making dissent impossible to
carry into future decisions.

That failure is not hypothetical. Any sufficiently complex platform can turn
administration into control. A migration drops older logs. A new policy
changes appeal semantics. A replacement implementation removes an export hook.
A secret-management layer authorizes a tool call but leaves the affected user
unable to inspect who used the authority and why. Each event can look like
ordinary maintenance unless the rights layer names it as a preservation
failure.

External alignment and governance literature helps bound the claim. The
corrigibility and off-switch sources show why human correction channels cannot
be treated as optional afterthoughts. Collective Constitutional AI gives a
public-input constitutional baseline, but one-time principle sourcing does not
replace runtime appeal, audit, exit, or fork interfaces. Contestable AI by
design gives a governance comparator for challenge and review, but this
repository has not implemented that framework or validated institutional
redress. These are comparators, not evidence that the ASI Stack already
provides legal rights, deployed portability, or real governance legitimacy.

The insufficiency is therefore precise: governance language without material
routes is not a right. It is an aspiration.

## Core Claim

Fork, exit, audit, dissent, and contestability should be treated as technical
governance interfaces, not only political ideals.

This remains an architectural argument. The live AI/research chapter keeps the
core claim at `argument` support. The source notes, schema, synthetic fixture
harness, and Lean module show that the project can model certain record
shapes and finite preservation gates. They do not prove institutional
governance rights, legal rights, deployed runtime enforcement, real fork or
exit usability, reviewer independence, source interpretation adequacy,
redaction quality, or deployed contestability.

The important word is "interface." An interface has a caller, a scope, an
artifact, a response, an error state, and a contract for what happens next. A
governance right should be held to the same standard.

## Mechanism

The rights layer creates a Governance Right Record whenever someone exercises
or preserves audit, exit, fork, appeal, or dissent.

The record names the right holder, the right type, the scope, the requested
material, the available material, the withheld material, the safety
constraints, the access path, the redaction or denial reason, the appeal path,
the expiry or revisit condition, the challenged-party independence rule, the
preservation obligation, and the receipt references.

That is the difference between a promise and a route. If audit is granted, the
record says which artifacts were made available. If material is redacted, the
record says what was withheld and why. If exit is requested, the record says
what portable bundle or snapshot exists. If a fork is unsafe, the record says
which safety, privacy, source, or obligation boundary blocked it and how the
request can be reviewed. If dissent is preserved, the record names how it will
travel into later governance decisions.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Request["Rights request"] --> Record["Governance Right Record"]
  Record --> Audit["Audit artifacts"]
  Record --> Exit["Exit/export route"]
  Record --> Fork["Fork boundary"]
  Record --> Dissent["Dissent channel"]
  Record --> Redaction["Withheld material and reason"]
  Audit --> Receipt["Rights receipt"]
  Exit --> Receipt
  Fork --> Receipt
  Dissent --> Receipt
  Redaction --> Receipt
  Receipt --> Check["Preservation check"]
  Check -- "usable and safe" --> Proceed["Transition may proceed"]
  Check -- "missing, unsafe, or captured" --> Review["Block, residualize, or escalate"]
  Proceed --> Ledger["Evidence and governance ledger"]
  Review --> Ledger
```

The preservation check is where rights become operational. A governance
transition may proceed only if the relevant rights survive it. Missing audit
paths, unsafe forks, redaction without appeal, removed protected rights, or
missing exit capability route to review or residualization rather than silent
promotion.

This layer also prevents capture by record custody. The challenged authority
can participate in review, but it cannot be the only keeper of the record,
denial reason, appeal path, or export state. A right controlled solely by the
party it constrains is too easy to erase.

## Interfaces

The minimum Governance Right Record carries these fields:

- `right_id`
- `right_type`
- `request_state`
- `holder`
- `scope`
- `required_artifacts`
- `material_available`
- `material_withheld`
- `safety_constraints`
- `access_path`
- `denial_or_redaction_reason`
- `appeal_path`
- `expiry_or_revisit`
- `challenged_party_independence`
- `preservation_rule`
- `preservation_obligation`
- `receipt_refs`
- `test_hook`
- `non_claims`

Governance issues rights. Memory, security, and execution layers produce the
artifacts that make rights inspectable. Stable Capability Fields preserve
rights across replacement. Evidence ledgers record whether the right was
materially usable rather than merely declared.

Rights outcomes need separate states. Access failure is not the same as
justified denial. Redacted access is not the same as full access. Partial
export is not the same as portable export. A contested decision is not the
same as preserved dissent. If all non-success states collapse into refusal,
the system cannot be audited.

The interface standard is material usability. A declared audit right is weak
if records are missing, unreadable, unauthenticated, or held only by the
challenged party. A declared exit right is weak if the export omits the state
needed to continue elsewhere. A declared fork right is dangerous if it
silently drops safety obligations, private data boundaries, source lineage, or
redaction constraints.

## Invariants

Audit records cannot be silently deleted.

Exit paths remain materially usable.

Fork rights do not bypass safety constraints.

The challenged authority cannot be the only keeper of the record, denial
reason, appeal path, or export state.

Rights attach to the governed relationship, not to one convenient
implementation. A user should not lose audit, appeal, export, or fork paths
because a model, memory system, policy engine, or steward agent was replaced.
If the replacement cannot carry the rights receipt forward, the replacement is
not ready for default use.

The safety side matters just as much. A right is not a permission to leak
secrets, expose private data, bypass source obligations, or copy dangerous
tools. A safe denial can be legitimate. The requirement is that denial becomes
an inspectable artifact rather than a disappearance.

## Failure modes

Governance capture occurs when one authority controls policy, logs, appeal,
and revision.

Data hostage-taking converts dependency into control. A person may technically
leave, but the system keeps the only usable memory, provenance, artifact
graph, or source context.

Opaque policy updates turn governance into a moving target. The user can no
longer tell which rules applied when the contested decision happened.

Rights theater is the polite version of the same failure. The system publishes
rights language, but logs, exports, redaction rules, appeal channels, and
preservation hooks are unavailable when the right would constrain the system.

Fork laundering is a special risk. A fork can preserve dissent, but it can
also become an excuse to strip safety obligations, privacy constraints, or
source lineage. Fork rights are valuable only when they carry obligations with
the portable state.

Review capture occurs when the appeal process exists but the record, evidence,
or denial reason is still controlled by the challenged party.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The smallest honest implementation is a `governance_right_record` schema plus
fixtures for audit, exit, fork, appeal, and dissent.

The fixture suite should include one complete audit response, one justified
redaction with an appeal path, one exit export with a portable state bundle,
one fork request blocked because safety obligations cannot transfer, one
preserved dissent record, and one invalid case where the challenged party is
the only keeper of the appeal path.

The current repository already has the schema, protocol fixture validation,
the synthetic governance-rights harness, and finite Lean gates for audit/exit
preservation, protected-right removal, constrained-fork review, redaction
appeal, and missing-exit residualization. Those are useful controls. They
validate record discipline in synthetic cases. They do not prove legal rights,
institutional quality, actual access-path availability, redaction adequacy,
deployed export behavior, real fork safety, or independent review.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is contestability infrastructure for a self-improving
stack.

In that endpoint, every high-impact governance transition carries rights
receipts. Policy updates preserve audit and appeal. Memory migrations preserve
exit and export continuity. Capability replacement preserves protected rights
rather than deleting old paths. Fork requests carry source, privacy, and safety
obligations. Dissent can survive a decision and remain visible to later
review, rollback, or constitutional revision.

The system is not merely transparent. It is contestable under pressure. It can
say what was requested, what was granted, what was withheld, why it was
withheld, who can review it, what survives transition, and what remains
residualized.

That endpoint is not implemented here. It requires public-safe traces, richer
fixtures, data-portability reviews, safety-limited fork scenarios, independent
review records, and runtime artifacts before any stronger support state would
be warranted. The current chapter names the architecture and the evidence path
without claiming the institution already exists.

## Summary

Governance Rights: Fork, Exit, and Audit turns contestability into an
engineering surface.

The chapter's discipline is simple: make the right callable, make the response
inspectable, make denial reviewable, make exit usable, make fork safe, and
make dissent difficult to erase.

The healthiest governance system is not one that never says no. It is one
that can say no with a record, a reason, a boundary, and an appeal path, while
preserving the rights that let people challenge or leave the system that made
the decision.

## Handoff

Rights need stable targets. Stable Capability Fields follows because audit,
exit, fork, rollback, and appeal cannot depend on a temporary implementation
name that disappears during replacement. Stable fields give capabilities
persistent identities, evaluator policies, evidence histories, and authority
ceilings so governance rights can attach to what a capability is supposed to
do, not merely to the current code path.
